meta.title: Occupational Health Institution Transformation Simulator
meta.description: This JSON file defines the staged institutional model, controls, presets and Monte Carlo scoring logic for a shell-based simulator of occupational health transformation. The HTML shell loads this file using a URL parameter such as ?data=oh_institution_data.json and then reproduces the full app behaviour.

controls.label: Workflow fragmentation / handoffs
controls.help: High values mean cases are split across intake, booking, consultation, QA and reporting.

controls.label: Template rigidity
controls.help: High values mean standardised reports suppress context-sensitive judgement.

controls.label: Non-value QA / admin burden
controls.help: High values mean report returns, mandatory fields and duplicated checks create internal failure demand.

controls.label: Local team capability and continuity
controls.help: Higher values mean the system handles variety earlier through local ownership and better coordination.

controls.label: Demand visibility / classification quality
controls.help: Higher values mean value demand, internally generated failure demand and externally conditioned demand are clearly distinguished.

controls.label: Client management capability
controls.help: Higher values mean better referrals, better implementation of advice, and less use of OH as a management substitute.

controls.label: External client operational pressure
controls.help: High values mean production pressure, legal anxiety, workload strain or organisational turbulence increase unavoidable demand.

controls.label: Contract flexibility for learning and redesign
controls.help: Higher values mean contracts allow collaborative redesign, upstream challenge and more realistic performance conversations.

controls.label: Trust with clients and internal stakeholders
controls.help: Higher trust reduces defensive behaviour and makes difficult change conversations more feasible.

controls.label: Throughput target pressure
controls.help: High values keep the system focused on volume and timeliness proxies rather than usefulness and outcome.

controls.label: Political resistance to redesign
controls.help: High values mean finance, sales, QA, operational managers or clients may block redesign.

controls.label: Governance / compliance anxiety
controls.help: High values mean regulation is interpreted defensively, adding controls that may generate more failure demand.

controls.label: Transformation mitigation strength
controls.help: Higher values mean pilots, demand evidence, joint reviews, protected governance and narrative reframing are active.

controls.label: Redesign effort and leadership commitment
controls.help: Higher values mean the organisation is actively redistributing variety, changing handoffs, and backing local capability.

stages.name: Transactional fragmentation
stages.description: Targets, handoffs and templates dominate; failure demand remains hidden.

stages.name: Demand visibility
stages.description: The provider begins classifying demand and seeing where waste is generated.

stages.name: Purpose-led redesign
stages.description: Variety is handled earlier through local capability, continuity and redesign.

stages.name: Negotiated transformation
stages.description: Old and new logics coexist; politics and mitigation determine whether change survives.

stages.name: Viable relational OH
stages.description: The service becomes more useful, less transactional and more capable of prevention and learning.

riskRules.title: Target distortion
riskRules.body: High throughput pressure is likely to force the service back toward transaction counts and SLA optics rather than usefulness, learning and outcome.

riskRules.title: Political rollback risk
riskRules.body: Finance, operations, QA or client actors are likely to contest redesign unless mitigation remains visible and active.

riskRules.title: Defensive compliance loop
riskRules.body: Governance may be interpreted as a reason to add controls, fields and approvals, increasing internal failure demand.

riskRules.title: Upstream client-generated demand
riskRules.body: Weak local management capability under pressure is likely to keep producing poor referrals, repeat cases and generic requests for OH advice.

riskRules.title: Contract rigidity
riskRules.body: Commercial commitments may limit the provider’s ability to redesign flow, challenge upstream demand or change what gets measured.

riskRules.title: Variety overload at the centre
riskRules.body: Too much complexity is being absorbed too late in the pathway, creating rework, queueing and clinician frustration.

riskRules.title: Kick-back is currently containable
riskRules.body: Resistance still exists, but the balance of trust, mitigation and redesign effort makes sustained transformation more plausible.

visibility.title: Strengthen demand visibility
visibility.body: Study incoming work from the point of view of managers and patients. Separate value demand from failure demand, and distinguish internal provider-generated demand from externally conditioned client demand.

redesign.title: Redistribute variety earlier
redesign.body: Reduce handoffs, strengthen local ownership, improve referral clarification, and remove non-value QA or template burdens so clinicians face better-shaped work.

politics.title: Stabilise the politics of change
politics.body: Use pilots, side-by-side evidence, shadow metrics and governance-protected redesign to stop finance, QA or operational anxiety from collapsing the change effort.

clientPath.title: Work upstream with clients
clientPath.body: Improve line-manager question quality, challenge the misuse of OH as a management substitute, and build more relational contract conversations where possible.

S2.title: Prevent dual-rule-set fatigue
S2.body: Do not ask teams to work in a purpose-led way while still judging them only through old throughput targets. Real transformation requires the accountability regime to shift too.

S3.title: Protect the redesign from rollback
S3.body: At this stage the redesign is politically visible. Maintain executive backing, visible demand evidence and explicit mitigation of governance and finance anxiety.

S4.title: Treat residual failure demand as a learning signal
S4.body: A viable service is not a perfect one. Monitor new shocks, client regression, contract drift and subtle re-transactionalisation pressures.

mechanismTexts.internal: function(v, summary){ return `High handoffs (${v.handoffs}), template rigidity (${v.templates}) and QA burden (${v.qaBurden}) are ${summary.failure.mean > 55 ? 'driving a large share of avoidable internal demand' : 'less dominant than in the baseline state'}. Raising local capability (${v.localCapability}) and redesign effort (${v.redesignEffort}) weakens this loop.`; }
mechanismTexts.client: function(v, summary){ return `External pressure (${v.externalPressure}) and client capability (${v.clientCapability}) shape how much of the incoming demand is genuinely external and how much can be influenced upstream. Contract flexibility (${v.contractFlex}) and trust (${v.relationalTrust}) determine whether the provider can challenge poor demand productively.`; }
mechanismTexts.politics: function(v, summary){ return `Political resistance (${v.politicalResistance}), governance anxiety (${v.governanceAnxiety}) and target pressure (${v.targetPressure}) ${summary.kickback.mean > 55 ? 'make rollback or symbolic transformation likely unless mitigation is strengthened.' : 'are present but currently more containable.'} Mitigation power (${v.mitigationPower}) and trust (${v.relationalTrust}) are the main stabilisers.`; }
